frameworks.name: signal-detection theory
frameworks.one_line: decomposes Vernier performance into sensitivity (d') and decision criterion components

frameworks.name: perceptual learning
frameworks.one_line: practice-driven performance improvement attributed to either sensitivity or criterion shifts

core_question: Do different feedback types in perceptual learning selectively affect sensitivity versus decision criterion, and do these changes differ in persistence?
key_assumptions: performance gains decompose into sensitivity and criterion components
criterion shifts are typically treated as nuisance rather than learning
manipulations: feedback type: block feedback vs trial-wise feedback
feedback bias: unbiased vs biased trial-wise feedback
key_findings: block feedback changes sensitivity but leaves criterion unchanged
criterion changes are confined to the training session and do not survive overnight
biased trial-wise feedback induces a sensitivity change that distorts perception (left offset perceived as right)
interpretations: sensitivity and criterion changes reflect dissociable learning mechanisms with different temporal stability
biased feedback can shift the perceptual encoding itself, not just response strategy
